data.body:
Severity: security medium. Source: TYB-12-followup security audit.

Where: server/internal/httpserver/checkout_handlers.go:80-234 (POST /api/checkout/start).

What: no auth, no rate limit. Each call:
1. Renders a PNG (server CPU + disk).
2. Creates a Printful sync_product (durable upstream resource).
3. Opens a Stripe Checkout Session (durable upstream resource).
Idempotency is keyed on a hash of (text, middletext) so iterating distinct inputs creates distinct upstream resources.

Impact: a scripted attacker can rack up tens of thousands of orphan Stripe sessions and Printful sync_products in minutes. Printful dashboard becomes unusable; Stripe analytics polluted; disk fills with rendered PNGs. Not directly fiscal, but operationally hostile and the cleanup is manual.

Recommendation: add a coarse rate limit. Specifically:
- per-IP token bucket: 10 requests per minute, burst 5.
- global cap: 1000 requests per hour.
- reject with 429 + a small JSON body when exceeded.
- in-memory only; this is not a distributed system. Use golang.org/x/time/rate for the token bucket and a sync.Map or simple mutex-guarded map keyed by client IP, with periodic eviction.
Also consider applying the same limiter to /api/render and /api/printful/products since they share the renderer + Printful product creation.

Effort: small. Addresses the trivial drive-by attack; targeted DDoS still needs upstream protection (out of scope).
